content.text:
A question I keep hearing from security teams:

"How do we audit what our AI systems are accessing?"

The honest answer for most RAG deployments: you can't.

Vector databases log queries, not permissions.
LLM APIs don't know your access model.
Application logs show requests, not authorization decisions.

This isn't a monitoring gap. It's a security gap.

Every retrieval your AI makes should answer:
→ Who asked?
→ What was returned?
→ Was access authorized?
→ What policy allowed/denied it?

If you can't answer these questions in under 5 minutes, you have a compliance problem waiting to happen.
content.headline: The AI Audit Question
content.cta: Learn how Gateco provides complete audit trails

thread:
🧵 The uncomfortable truth about AI security in enterprises:

Most security teams have no idea what their AI systems are accessing.

Here's why this is a bigger problem than you think 👇

1/ Your IAM policies are comprehensive.

Users have roles. Roles have permissions. Permissions map to resources.

But when a user asks your AI copilot a question, those policies don't apply to the vector retrieval.

2/ Vector databases retrieve by semantic similarity.

They don't know:
- Who is asking
- What they're allowed to see
- Whether the content is sensitive

They just return the closest matches.

3/ The result:

A contractor can query executive compensation.
A departing employee can access current roadmaps.
A junior dev can retrieve security audit findings.

The AI just... answers.

4/ The fix isn't prompting. You can't "ask nicely" for the LLM to respect permissions it doesn't know about.

The fix is authorization at the retrieval layer.

Before content reaches the LLM, not after.

5/ This is what permission-aware retrieval does:

✓ Identity checked on every query
✓ Policy evaluated per chunk
✓ Only authorized content returned
✓ Every decision logged

Same question + different user = different authorized answer.

entries.notes: Week 1: Main thread - Security gap deep dive

content.text:
Your security team blocks USB drives.
They monitor email attachments.
They scan cloud uploads.

But they don't audit what your AI retrieves.

This is the new exfiltration surface that most enterprises haven't addressed.

An employee with access to an AI copilot can effectively query your entire knowledge base—even content they wouldn't normally have access to—just by asking the right questions.

The vector database doesn't check permissions.
It just finds semantically similar content.

This isn't a hypothetical. It's happening in production AI systems today.

The question isn't whether to address it. It's when.
content.headline: The New Exfiltration Surface
content.cta: See how to close this gap

content.text:
Your company blocks USB drives but doesn't audit AI retrieval.

One of these is a bigger risk in 2026.

Hint: it's not the USB drive.

I talked to a CISO last week who said:

"We paused our RAG deployment because we couldn't answer basic security questions."

The questions they couldn't answer:

1. Can a user in Sales access HR documents through the AI?
2. What happens when someone's role changes?
3. How do we prove to auditors who accessed what?
4. Can we prevent the AI from surfacing M&A documents to non-deal-team members?

These aren't edge cases. They're table stakes for enterprise deployment.

If your RAG system can't answer these questions, you don't have a production-ready AI system. You have a liability.
content.headline: Questions That Pause RAG Deployments
content.cta: Learn how to answer these questions

content.text:
Questions that should be easy to answer about your RAG system:

☐ Can Sales access HR docs via AI?

How many can you answer today?

Poll: When you deployed RAG internally, how did you handle document-level permissions?

Genuinely curious how teams are approaching this.

Drop your approach in the comments—no judgment, just learning from each other.
content.headline: RAG Permissions Poll

content.text:
How permission-aware retrieval actually works:

Step 1: QUERY
User asks AI a question. Gateco intercepts the retrieval request.

Step 2: RETRIEVE
Vector DB returns top-K semantically similar chunks.
(This is standard RAG.)

Step 3: EVALUATE
For each chunk, Gateco evaluates:
→ Who is asking? (Principal)
→ What are they accessing? (Resource)
→ What's the action? (Retrieve)
→ What does policy say? (RBAC/ABAC/ReBAC)

Step 4: FILTER
Only authorized chunks pass through.
Unauthorized chunks are blocked.

Step 5: LOG
Every decision recorded: who, what, when, verdict.

Result: Same question, different authorized answers.

Latency impact: milliseconds, not seconds.
content.headline: Permission-Aware Retrieval: How It Works

thread:
🧵 How to integrate permission-aware retrieval in 10 minutes:

A quick walkthrough for anyone building RAG systems 👇

1/ Install the SDK:

pip install gateco

Or for JS/TS:
npm install @gateco/client

2/ Initialize with your API key:

from gateco import GatecoClient

client = GatecoClient(
    api_key="gco_..."
)

3/ Replace your direct vector DB call:

# Before
results = vector_db.query(embedding, top_k=10)

# After
results = client.retrieve(
    query=user_query,
    user_id=current_user.id,
    top_k=10
)

4/ That's it. Seriously.

Gateco handles:
✓ Policy evaluation
✓ User identity verification
✓ Chunk-level filtering
✓ Audit logging

5/ Works with:
- Pinecone
- pgvector
- Weaviate
- Chroma
- OpenSearch

Docs: https://gateco.io/docs

#RAGSecurity

content.text:
RBAC vs ABAC vs ReBAC for AI retrieval:

Which policy model should you use?

🔵 RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)
"Engineers can access engineering docs"

Best for: Simple hierarchies, department-based access
Limitation: Can't handle dynamic conditions

🟢 ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control)
"Access if user.clearance >= document.classification"

Best for: Fine-grained rules, environmental context
Limitation: Complex policy authoring

🟠 ReBAC (Relationship-Based Access Control)
"Access if user is member of document.project"

Best for: Collaborative workspaces, org structures
Limitation: Requires relationship graph

🔹 The answer?

Usually a combination.

Gateco supports all three—evaluated at query time, not baked into embeddings.

Mix and match based on your org's needs.
content.headline: RBAC vs ABAC vs ReBAC for AI

content.text:
Quick poll for AI engineers:

What policy model do you use (or want to use) for RAG access control?

🔵 RBAC (role-based)
🟢 ABAC (attribute-based)
🟠 ReBAC (relationship-based)
🔴 Combination / Other

Integrating Gateco with LangChain:

For anyone building RAG with LangChain, here's how to add permission-aware retrieval:

```python
from langchain.retrievers import GatecoRetriever
from gateco import GatecoClient

# Initialize Gateco
client = GatecoClient(api_key="gco_...")

# Create retriever
retriever = GatecoRetriever(
    gateco_client=client,
    user_context=lambda: get_current_user()
)

# Use in your chain
chain = RetrievalQA.from_chain_type(
    llm=your_llm,
    retriever=retriever
)
```

Every query now:
→ Checks user identity
→ Evaluates access policies
→ Filters unauthorized chunks
→ Logs the retrieval decision

Same LangChain patterns. Added security layer.
content.headline: Gateco + LangChain Integration

content.text:
LangChain + Gateco = permission-aware RAG

Swap your retriever, keep your chain.

Every query:
✓ Identity checked
✓ Policy evaluated
✓ Unauthorized chunks filtered
✓ Decision logged

Docs: https://gateco.io/docs/langchain

"Won't adding a permission layer slow down retrieval?"

A fair question. Here's the honest answer:

Latency impact: 5-15ms per retrieval

📊 Benchmarks (p95):
→ Direct vector DB: ~50ms
→ + Gateco policy eval: ~60ms
→ + Audit logging: ~65ms

Why it's fast:
1. Policy evaluation is optimized (precompiled rules)
2. No additional DB calls needed
3. Async audit logging
4. CDN-distributed policy cache

The real question:

Is 15ms worth knowing who accessed what, with enterprise-grade audit trails?

For production enterprise deployments, the answer is always yes.
content.headline: Performance Impact: The Honest Answer

content.text:
SOC 2 auditors are adding AI access to their scope.

Here's what they're asking:

☐ How do AI systems access customer data?
☐ What data can AI systems retrieve?
☐ Who can query customer data via AI interfaces?
☐ How are AI access decisions logged?
☐ Can you demonstrate access controls for AI retrieval?

If your RAG system accesses data covered by SOC 2, you need to answer these questions.

"The AI just retrieves semantically similar content" is not a compliance-ready answer.

AI access is still access.
It needs the same controls.
It needs the same audit trails.

The good news: this is a solvable problem.
content.headline: SOC 2 and AI Access

thread:
🧵 SOC 2 auditors are starting to ask about AI.

If you're SOC 2 compliant, here's what you need to know about RAG systems 👇

1/ AI access = data access

When your RAG system retrieves content, that's data access.

If the underlying data is in SOC 2 scope, the AI retrieval is too.

2/ What auditors want to see:

✓ Access controls on AI retrieval
✓ Logging of AI access decisions
✓ Audit trail: who queried what, when
✓ Evidence that permissions are enforced

3/ What most RAG systems provide:

❌ None of the above

Vector DBs don't log per-document access.
LLM APIs don't know your permission model.
Application logs don't capture authorization decisions.

4/ The fix:

Add a permission-aware retrieval layer that:
→ Enforces access policies on every query
→ Logs every retrieval decision
→ Maps to your existing identity model

AI access. Treated like any other access.

#SOC2 #AICompliance

content.text:
A healthcare company built an AI assistant for their clinical staff.

The use case made sense: Help clinicians quickly find relevant protocols, research, and patient guidelines.

The problem: The vector database didn't differentiate between:
→ Research papers (available to all)
→ Internal protocols (clinical staff only)
→ Patient-specific guidelines (care team only)

A billing coordinator could ask about specific patient treatment protocols and get answers.

Not because the billing coordinator was malicious.
But because the AI didn't know the difference.

The fix wasn't turning off the AI.
It was adding permission-aware retrieval.

Same AI. Same knowledge base. Proper HIPAA boundaries.
content.headline: Healthcare RAG: A Compliance Story

content.text:
Healthcare AI + RAG = HIPAA questions

If a billing coordinator can query clinical protocols through your AI, that's a HIPAA concern.

The AI doesn't know the difference between "available to all" and "care team only."

Your retrieval layer should.

Financial services firms are deploying AI assistants for:
→ Research analysts
→ Relationship managers
→ Compliance teams

The challenge: The same AI, accessing the same knowledge base, needs different boundaries for each user.

A research analyst shouldn't access client-specific portfolio details.
A relationship manager shouldn't access compliance investigation notes.
A compliance officer needs to see everything—but only within their scope.

Metadata filters aren't enough.
Prompt-level restrictions are bypassable.

What you need: identity-aware retrieval.

The query stays the same.
The answer changes based on who's asking.

That's how enterprise AI should work.
content.headline: AI for Financial Services: The Access Challenge

content.text:
Financial services AI access control:

Research analyst ≠ relationship manager ≠ compliance officer

Same AI. Same query. Different authorized answers.

If your RAG system treats them all the same, you have a compliance problem.

The legal industry has strict rules about information barriers.

When a law firm represents competing clients:
→ Teams must be separated
→ Documents must be isolated
→ Communications must be tracked

These "ethical walls" are fundamental to legal practice.

Now add an AI assistant.

Without permission-aware retrieval, that AI can access documents from both matters. The "wall" has a massive hole in it.

The fix isn't removing the AI—it's ensuring the AI respects the same boundaries that apply to everyone else.

Matter-level isolation.
Role-based access.
Complete audit trail.

AI should make lawyers more efficient, not create new liability.
content.headline: AI and Legal Information Barriers

content.headline: April Wrap-Up

content.text:
The most common question I get about RAG security:

"Can't we just filter results after the fact?"

Short answer: It's better than nothing, but not ideal.

Longer answer:

Post-retrieval filtering means:
→ Unauthorized content still gets retrieved
→ Content reaches memory/logs before filtering
→ Top-K results may not include enough authorized content
→ Performance hit from retrieving more than needed

Permission-aware retrieval:
→ Unauthorized content never retrieved
→ Policy evaluated before content returned
→ Top-K always reflects authorized results
→ Efficient retrieval without over-fetching

Both filter. One is architecturally sound.
content.headline: Pre-Filter vs Post-Filter

content.text:
"Can't we just filter after retrieval?"

You can, but:

• Unauthorized content still gets retrieved
• Content hits memory before filtering
• Top-K may not include enough valid results

Better: filter at retrieval time, not after.

entries.notes: Quick insight - Pre vs post filter

Why this matters:

Gateco mirrors your existing identity model. We don't replace your IdP—we integrate with it.

When a user queries your RAG system:

Your IdP. Your users. Now enforced on AI retrieval.

Looking ahead: AI security trends for the rest of 2026.

What we're seeing:

1. Compliance audits expanding to AI systems
SOC 2, HIPAA, and GDPR scope increasingly includes AI-powered access.

2. "Shadow AI" becoming a security concern
Employees using AI tools on corporate data without oversight.

3. AI security as a board-level conversation
Not just IT concern anymore—exec teams want answers.

4. Shift from "prompt security" to "retrieval security"
Recognition that prompts are suggestions, retrieval is enforcement.

5. Multi-agent systems requiring multi-tenant security
As AI agents proliferate, so do access control requirements.

The organizations getting ahead of this are implementing security-by-design, not security-as-afterthought.

Which trends are you seeing?
